content.problemStatement:
Chef has NNN numbers written on his board. He wants only one number to remain on the board and so he devises a special tactic to do so.

He randomly picks up 222 numbers written on the board say AAA and BBB respectively. He erases both of them from the board and writes a new number on the board which equals -


A‚ąíBA - BA‚ąíB if parity of AAA === parity of BBB
A+BA + BA+B if parity of AAA ‚Č†\neqÓÄ†= parity of BBB
Here parity refers to remainder obtained when a number is divided by 222.

Notice that the total numbers on the board reduced by 111 after 111 such operation. After a finite number of operations there would only be 111 number remaining on the board.

Chef is interested in knowing how many different final numbers he could end up with.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer NNN denoting the amount of numbers initially present on the board.
The second line consists of NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,...,ANA_1, A_2,..., A_NA1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,...,AN‚Äč denoting the numbers written on the board initially.


Output Format
For each testcase, output a single integer denoting the number of possible final numbers on the board.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer N, the number of integers on the board. The second line contains N space‚ÄĎseparated integers A1 ‚Ä¶ AN.
content.outputFormat: A single integer ‚Äď the number of distinct values that can be the last remaining number after performing the described operations any number of times.

solution.approach:
The operation reduces the size of the multiset by one. For a given multiset we can try every ordered pair (i, j) (i != j). If Ai and Aj have the same parity we replace them by (Ai - Aj); otherwise we replace them by (Ai + Aj). Recursively continue until only one number remains. All reachable final numbers are stored in a global set. Because the number of elements shrinks by one each step, the total depth of recursion is N‚ÄĎ1. The state of the recursion is completely described by the multiset of current numbers; we store visited states (sorted vector) in a memoisation set to avoid recomputation. The overall number of distinct states for N ‚Č§ 12 is comfortably small, so the brute‚ÄĎforce with memoisation finishes well within limits.

Key points:
1. Represent a state as a sorted vector of integers and convert it to a string key for the memo set.
2. For each state, iterate over all ordered pairs (i, j). Compute the new value according to the parity rule, build the next state (remove the two used numbers, insert the new one), sort it and recurse.
3. When the state size becomes 1, insert that single value into the answer set.
4. After the whole search finishes, the answer is the size of the answer set.

Complexities:
- Time: O(number_of_states * N^2). For N ‚Č§ 12 the number of states is at most a few hundred thousand.
- Space: O(number_of_states * N) for memoisation plus O(N) recursion depth.
solution.timeComplexity: Approximately O(S * N^2) where S is the number of distinct reachable states (for N ‚Č§ 12, S ‚Č§ 2¬∑10^5).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(S * N) for memoisation plus O(N) recursion stack.
